symbols.name: M_a
symbols.def: M_a is the mediator under do(X=a), for a in B

symbols.name: Y^M_m
symbols.def: Y^M_m is the outcome under do(M=m), for m in B

symbols.name: Y^X_a
symbols.def: Y^X_a=Y^M_{M_a}, for a in B

symbols.name: p
symbols.def: p=(p_{xmy})_{(x,m,y) in B^3}, where p_{xmy}=Pr(M=m,Y=y | do(X=x))

symbols.name: q
symbols.def: q=(q_r)_{r in R}, where q_r=Pr((M_0,M_1,Y^M_0,Y^M_1)=r)
symbols.role: joint response-type law allowing arbitrary M-Y confounding

symbols.name: A
symbols.def: A_{(x,m,y),r}=1{m=m_x and y=y_{m_x}}

symbols.name: B_A
symbols.role: rank-reduced map encoding all eight cells with arm normalization

symbols.name: D
symbols.def: D={r in R:m_0=1 and m_1=0}

symbols.name: Q_j(p)
symbols.def: Q_j(p)={q in Delta(R):B_A q=b(p), and (j=0 or q_r=0 for every r in D)} for j in B
symbols.role: sign-free and mediator-monotone compatible response-type polytopes

symbols.name: P_j
symbols.def: P_j={p in [0,1]^8: sum_{m,y in B}p_{xmy}=1 for every x in B, d(p)>0, and Q_j(p) is nonempty} for j in B

symbols.name: V_j^-
symbols.def: V_j^-=vert({w in R^7:(B_A^T w)_r<=c_r for every r in R if j=0, and for every r in R minus D if j=1}) for j in B

symbols.name: V_j^+
symbols.def: V_j^+=vert({w in R^7:(B_A^T w)_r>=c_r for every r in R if j=0, and for every r in R minus D if j=1}) for j in B

symbols.name: L_j(p)
symbols.def: L_j(p)=max_{w in V_j^-} w^T b(p)/d(p), for j in B

symbols.name: U_j(p)
symbols.def: U_j(p)=min_{w in V_j^+} w^T b(p)/d(p), for j in B

symbols.name: G(p)
symbols.def: G(p)=(G_L(p),G_U(p))

symbols.name: lambda_0^-(p)
symbols.def: lambda_0^-(p)=min{c^Tq:q in Q_0(p)}

symbols.name: lambda_0^+(p)
symbols.def: lambda_0^+(p)=max{c^Tq:q in Q_0(p)}

symbols.name: S^-(p)
symbols.def: S^-(p)=min{sum_{r in D}q_r:q in Q_0(p), c^Tq=lambda_0^-(p)}

symbols.name: S^+(p)
symbols.def: S^+(p)=min{sum_{r in D}q_r:q in Q_0(p), c^Tq=lambda_0^+(p)}

symbols.name: n
symbols.def: n is the number of observed trial records

symbols.name: O_i
symbols.def: O_i=(X_i,M_i,Y_i), for i in {1,...,n}

symbols.name: hat p_n
symbols.def: On E_n, hat p_{n,xmy}=sum_{i=1}^n 1{O_i=(x,m,y)} / sum_{i=1}^n 1{X_i=x}, for (x,m,y) in B^3
symbols.role: randomized-arm empirical cell law on the nonempty-arms event

symbols.name: Z
symbols.def: Z is the Gaussian limit of sqrt(n)(hat p_n-p)

symbols.name: h
symbols.def: h is a directional increment in the randomized cell-law coordinates

symbols.name: Phi'_p
symbols.def: Phi'_p is the Hadamard directional derivative of Phi at p

symbols.name: N_{n,x}
symbols.def: N_{n,x}=sum_{i=1}^n 1{X_i=x}, for x in B

symbols.name: xi_i^*
symbols.def: Conditional on O_1,...,O_n, xi_1^*,...,xi_n^* are independent N(0,1) variables, for i in {1,...,n}

symbols.name: hat Z_n^*
symbols.def: On E_n, hat Z_{n,xmy}^*=sqrt(n)N_{n,x}^{-1}sum_{i=1}^n xi_i^*1{X_i=x}[1{(M_i,Y_i)=(m,y)}-hat p_{n,xmy}], for (x,m,y) in B^3

symbols.name: widehat Phi'_{n,a_n}
symbols.def: widehat Phi'_{n,a_n} is obtained from the finite primal-dual basis enumeration in def:threshold-handle and the dual envelopes in def:dual-envelope by evaluating every resulting smooth selection formula at hat p_n, retaining at each max node every selection within a_n of its largest value and at each min node every selection within a_n of its smallest value, and recursively applying the corresponding max/min of the analytic directional derivatives to an increment h
symbols.role: computable estimated directional derivative with near-tie correction

symbols.name: W_n^*
symbols.def: On E_n intersect {hat p_n in P_1}, W_n^*=widehat Phi'_{n,a_n}(hat Z_n^*), and W_n^*=(0,0,0,0,0)^T otherwise

symbols.name: hat Phi_n
symbols.def: hat Phi_n=Phi(hat p_n) on E_n intersect {hat p_n in P_1}, and hat Phi_n=(0,0,0,0,0)^T otherwise

symbols.name: alpha
symbols.def: alpha is the nominal joint confidence-set error probability

symbols.name: hat c_{n,1-alpha}
symbols.def: hat c_{n,1-alpha} is the conditional (1-alpha)-quantile of ||W_n^*||_infinity given O_1,...,O_n

symbols.name: C_{n,1-alpha}
symbols.def: C_{n,1-alpha}={v in R^5:sqrt(n)||hat Phi_n-v||_infinity<=hat c_{n,1-alpha}}
symbols.role: bootstrap joint confidence region for Phi(p)

assumptions.condition: Y^X_a=Y^M_{M_a} for every a in B.

novel.justification: This restriction is credible for interventions that only make a binary mechanism more available, such as an encouragement that can activate but cannot suppress access to a mediator; it permits arbitrary dependence between that response and both outcome responses.

assumptions.condition: O_1,...,O_n are independent draws from the law of O_1.
Pr(X=x)>0 for every x in B.

definitions.name: canonical response-type construction
definitions.construction: Draw r=(m_0,m_1,y_0,y_1) from q, set M=m_X, and set Y=y_M.

definitions.name: full-law response-type construction
definitions.construction: For each j in B and p in P_j, form Q_j(p)={q in Delta(R):B_Aq=b(p), (j=0 or q_r=0 for every r in D)}.

definitions.name: canonical dual-envelope construction
definitions.construction: For each j in B, enumerate V_j^- and V_j^+ and evaluate w^Tb(p)/d(p) over their members to form L_j(p) and U_j(p).

definitions.name: defier-support-slack threshold handle
definitions.construction: For each p in P_1, solve the two sign-free numerator programs defining lambda_0^-(p) and lambda_0^+(p), then minimize sum_{r in D}q_r over each corresponding equality-constrained optimal face of Q_0(p) to obtain S^-(p), S^+(p), and T(p). Enumerate the feasible primal bases of these four linear programs to derive their irredundant piecewise-affine representations.

definitions.name: tie-aware bootstrap construction
definitions.construction: Set a_n=n^{-1/4}; on E_n draw xi_1^*,...,xi_n^* conditionally independently from N(0,1), form hat Z_n^*, enumerate all dual and support-slack primal bases, construct widehat Phi'_{n,a_n} by the stated a_n near-tie max/min rule, and resample W_n^*=widehat Phi'_{n,a_n}(hat Z_n^*). Set hat c_{n,1-alpha} to its conditional sup-norm quantile and report C_{n,1-alpha}.

statements.statement: For every p in P_0, under ass:random-assignment, ass:complete-mediation, and ass:pn-positivity, the identified set of PN over def:canonical-scm laws matching p is exactly [L_0(p),U_0(p)]. Both endpoints are attained by elements of Q_0(p), and the displayed finite dual envelopes are max/min-of-affine full-law functionals divided by d(p).
statements.justification: This is the sharp benchmark required to distinguish substantive information from a merely formal monotonicity restriction.
statements.gap: Tian and Pearl (2000) derive sharp bounds for PN without exploiting a measured confounded mediator; Dawid, Murtas, and Musio (2014) obtain mediator PC bounds only after removing mediator-outcome confounding.
statements.consumer: The interval is the attribution benchmark for randomized mechanism studies with arbitrary common causes of M and Y.

statements.statement: For every p in P_1, under ass:random-assignment, ass:complete-mediation, ass:pn-positivity, and ass:mediator-monotonicity, the identified set of PN is exactly [L_1(p),U_1(p)]. Both endpoints are attained by elements of Q_1(p), and Q_1(p) is the face of Q_0(p) with q_r=0 for every r in D.
statements.justification: The theorem translates the no-defiers restriction into a sharp interval that respects every randomized mediator-outcome cell.
statements.gap: Dawid, Murtas, and Musio (2014) exclude M-Y confounding, and Gabriel, Sachs, and Sjolander (2023) bound population risk-difference decompositions rather than individual PN.
statements.consumer: It supplies an attribution range when a scientific monotonicity claim is credible but sequential ignorability is not.

statements.statement: For every p in P_1, derive irredundant finite piecewise-affine representations of the primitive support slacks S^-(p) and S^+(p), and prove that T(p)=min{S^-(p),S^+(p)}>0 if and only if both endpoint inequalities hold: L_1(p)>L_0(p) and U_1(p)<U_0(p). Derive closed-form expressions for G_L(p) and G_U(p), prove that S^-(p)>0 is equivalent to G_L(p)>0 and S^+(p)>0 is equivalent to G_U(p)>0, certify that each positive slack excludes every sign-free endpoint-attaining coupling from the defier-free face, and exhibit a nonempty relatively open subset of P_1 on which both slacks are positive.
statements.justification: The support slack is a primitive observable LP over the sign-free extremal face, so it tests whether that face intersects the no-defier face without defining a threshold from the endpoint gaps.
statements.gap: No scanned result gives an observable necessary-and-sufficient two-endpoint strict-gain condition for mediator monotonicity under arbitrary M-Y confounding. Xie and Li (2026, Theorem 3.3) formulate mediator-informed PNS optimization with a back-door set and the conditional-independence constraints in their equations (14)-(15); those constraints are absent here, and their program does not characterize the monotonicity defier-face frontier. Dawid, Humphreys, and Musio (2024) bounds PC in specified complete-mediator process models, while Kawakami and Tian (2025) identifies path-specific PNS measures; neither studies this frontier.
statements.consumer: It lets an analyst determine whether imposing no mediator defiers produces actual attribution information at both PN endpoints rather than a vacuous reparameterization.

statements.statement: For every p in the relative interior of P_1, under ass:iid-trial-sampling and ass:assignment-positivity, Pr(E_n intersect {hat p_n in P_1}) tends to one and sqrt(n)(hat Phi_n-Phi(p)) converges in distribution to Phi'_p(Z). Conditional on E_n intersect {hat p_n in P_1}, the conditional law of the explicit multiplier statistic W_n^* in def:tie-aware-bootstrap-handle consistently estimates that limit at ties among active dual and support-slack bases; consequently Pr(Phi(p) in C_{n,1-alpha}) tends to 1-alpha, conditional on oeq:canonical-defier-threshold yielding the stated finite directional-differentiable representation of T.
statements.justification: The two-endpoint strictness question is empirically most consequential at active-face ties, where an ordinary smooth delta method is invalid.
statements.gap: Zhang, Wang, and Miao (2025) derive efficient inference for sharp PN bounds in randomized treatment-outcome data with optional covariates under a margin condition; their bound has neither the full confounded mediator law nor the paired monotone/sign-free endpoint-and-support-slack map. Fang and Santos (2019) provides the generic directional-delta route for the finite-envelope regime retained here.
statements.consumer: It supports joint uncertainty statements about both endpoint tightening and the two-endpoint strictness threshold in a randomized mechanism trial.

estimand_functional: PN in [L_j(p),U_j(p)], where L_j(p)=max_{w in V_j^-}w^Tb(p)/d(p) and U_j(p)=min_{w in V_j^+}w^Tb(p)/d(p), j in {0,1}

sampling_model.treatment: X_i in B with positive arm probabilities
sampling_model.overlap: Pr(X=x)>0 for x in B and d(p)>0
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: None; on nonempty arms hat p_n is the armwise empirical cell law, while hat Phi_n uses its stated default outside the empirical compatibility event.

tldr: The project studies partial identification of treatment-control causal attribution in a randomized binary complete-mediation model in which the mediator and outcome may share arbitrary unobserved causes. It derives sharp sign-free and no-mediator-defier PN intervals from the entire experimental mediator-outcome law, then asks when deleting mediator defiers strictly contracts both endpoints. The proposed observable frontier is a pair of primitive support slacks: the least defier mass among sign-free lower- and upper-endpoint couplings. A tie-aware multiplier procedure with an explicit near-tie derivative map translates this finite response-type geometry to empirical use.

project_justification.gap: Existing PC mediation bounds either remove mediator-outcome confounding or point-identify different mediated quantities through cross-world assumptions; generic and partial-diagram PN programs do not identify the observable regimes in which mediator monotonicity has strict identifying content under arbitrary mediator-outcome response dependence.
project_justification.niche: The model preserves randomized treatment and complete mediation while allowing the unresolved mediator-outcome dependence that makes the sharp treatment-control PN response couplings consequential.
project_justification.fill: Finite response types yield sharp full-law partial-identification envelopes, and a primitive extremal-face support-slack analysis yields the two-endpoint strictness threshold, endpoint-gap formulas, an open two-endpoint-strictness region, and nonsmooth inference.

related_work: Tian and Pearl (2000) establish sharp probabilities-of-causation bounds under weak data assumptions, but do not use an observed confounded mediator or analyze a mediator-response restriction. Dawid, Murtas, and Musio (2014) show why observing a complete mediator can refine PC bounds, while their no-confounding setup leaves the present response-type coupling unresolved. Murtas, Dawid, and Musio (2017) extend mediation PC bounds under additional structure but do not characterize the defier-face gain under arbitrary mediator-outcome confounding. Dawid, Humphreys, and Musio (2024) derive PC bounds from known complete-mediator process structures, not from the arbitrary mediator-outcome-confounded response polytope used here. Gabriel, Sachs, and Sjolander (2023) supply a canonical-confounder sharp-bound template for mediated risk-difference decompositions, not treatment-control PN. Xie and Li (2026, Theorem 3.3) formulate mediator-informed PNS optimization using a back-door set and conditional-independence constraints; the present model permits arbitrary M-Y response dependence and instead characterizes the two-endpoint strict effect of deleting mediator defiers. Balke and Pearl (1997) supply the finite response-function and LP sharpness method adapted here. Fang and Santos (2019) supply the modified-bootstrap logic required by a nonsmooth finite envelope. Zhang, Wang, and Miao (2025) obtain efficient inference for sharp PN bounds from randomized treatment-outcome data with optional covariates under a margin condition; their result neither uses P(M,Y|do(X)) nor compares the monotone and sign-free mediator response polytopes. Rubinstein, Cuellar, and Malinsky (2025) point-identify mediated probabilities under strong cross-world conditions; the present target instead retains weak assumptions and delivers a sharp treatment-control attribution interval. Kawakami and Tian (2025) identify controlled and natural path-specific PNS measures, whereas this project bounds treatment-control PN without sequential ignorability and asks when mediator monotonicity has observable strict identifying content.
honest_scope: The two sharp interval theorems and the directional-delta route are finite-dimensional consequences to establish. The publishable open content is the irredundant observable support-slack frontier, the equivalence between its endpoint-specific slacks and positive endpoint gains, the coupling certificate, and a relatively open regime with strict contraction at both endpoints. The inference theorem is conditional on this frontier supplying the finite directional-differentiable threshold representation. The result does not identify mediated probabilities of causation under sequential ignorability and does not cover direct effects of X on Y.

bibliography.citation: Tian, J. and J. Pearl (2000). Probabilities of causation: Bounds and identification. Annals of Mathematics and Artificial Intelligence 28, 287-313.
Dawid, A. P., R. Murtas, and M. Musio (2014). Bounding the probability of causation in mediation analysis. arXiv:1411.2636.
Murtas, R., A. P. Dawid, and M. Musio (2017). New bounds for the probability of causation in mediation analysis. arXiv:1706.04857.
Balke, A. and J. Pearl (1997). Bounds on treatment effects from studies with imperfect compliance. Journal of the American Statistical Association 92, 1171-1176.
Gabriel, E. E., M. C. Sachs, and A. Sjolander (2023). Sharp nonparametric bounds for decomposition effects with two binary mediators. Journal of the American Statistical Association 118, 2135-2147.
Fang, Z. and A. Santos (2019). Inference on directionally differentiable functions. Review of Economic Studies 86, 377-412.
Zhang, P., R. Wang, and W. Miao (2025). Statistical inference for the probability of necessity for causal attribution. arXiv:2504.08294.
Rubinstein, M., M. Cuellar, and D. Malinsky (2025). Mediated probabilities of causation. Journal of Causal Inference 13, 20240019.
Dawid, P., M. Humphreys, and M. Musio (2024). Bounding causes of effects with mediators. Sociological Methods & Research 53, 28-56.
Kawakami, Y. and J. Tian (2025). Mediation analysis for probabilities of causation. Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 39, 26823-26832.
Chernozhukov, V., S. Lee, and A. M. Rosen (2013). Intersection bounds: Estimation and inference. Econometrica 81, 667-737.
Xie, Y. and A. Li (2026). Bounding probabilities of causation with partial causal diagrams. arXiv:2602.14503.

comparator_promise_table.comparator_claim: Derives theoretically sharp bounds on probabilities of causation from experimental and observational data under minimal assumptions.
Bounds probability of causation with an observed mediator under no direct effect and no confounding.
Develops new probability-of-causation bounds using partially mediating structure.
Obtains sharp bounds for mediated risk-difference decompositions with binary mediators and unmeasured mediator-outcome confounding.
Provides an alternative bootstrap for directionally differentiable maps when the standard bootstrap fails.
Derives efficient inference for sharp PN bounds in randomized treatment-outcome data with optional covariates under a margin condition.
Defines mediated probabilities of causation and identifies them under sufficient cross-world conditions.
Derives probability-of-causation bounds from specified complete-mediator process structures and mediator data patterns.
Identifies controlled, natural direct, and natural indirect probabilities of necessity and sufficiency under its mediation assumptions.
Uses response functions and linear programming to obtain tight bounds under imperfect compliance.
Theorem 3.3 bounds PNS, with PN obtained by changing the objective, through a mediator program with a back-door set and conditional-independence constraints in equations (14)-(15).

seed_details.red_team: Immediate from Dawid et al. because a mediator was already observed.
seed_details.rebuttal: Their reported bound assumes away mediator-outcome confounding, whereas q retains arbitrary dependence between mediator and outcome response coordinates.

seed_details.red_team: Immediate from Tian and Pearl because monotonicity only deletes response types.
seed_details.rebuttal: Generic PN bounds do not identify the observed-law regimes in which that deletion removes an endpoint-attaining confounded-mediator coupling.

seed_details.red_team: This is bookkeeping about redundant LP constraints.
seed_details.rebuttal: The proposed map must certify, endpoint by endpoint, which full-law cells exclude a latent coupling; it is retained only as a component of the strictness frontier.

seed_details.red_team: Direct application of Fang and Santos to a finite maximum.
seed_details.rebuttal: The PN normalization, joint monotone/sign-free envelope, and compatibility-face transitions require specialized derivatives, but this is an inference rung rather than the kernel.

seed_details.red_team: A routine sensitivity parameter for monotonicity violations.
seed_details.rebuttal: A critical defier-mass threshold could be new, but it is less direct than the observable exact frontier and is not selected.

seed_details.red_team: Different estimands make the comparison cosmetic.
seed_details.rebuttal: The targets differ, so it cannot deliver a strict comparator theorem; it is dropped.

seed_details.red_team: Replace their risk difference by PN in the same enumeration.
seed_details.rebuttal: The selected conditioning event makes PN a different response loading, but enumeration alone lacks the frontier object; retained only as technique support.

seed_details.red_team: Any strict LP example has a nearby strict example by continuity.
seed_details.rebuttal: Compatibility and endpoint-active faces can change at the boundary, so an explicit relatively open compatible region and complete latent witness remain necessary.

literature_map.anchor: Tian and Pearl (2000) supplies the weak-assumption PN benchmark; its sharpness result does not contain an observed confounded mediator response restriction.
adjacent_threads: Dawid, Murtas, and Musio (2014) motivates mediator information for PC but assumes no confounding.
Murtas, Dawid, and Musio (2017) develops new mediation PC bounds under stronger mediation structure.
Dawid, Humphreys, and Musio (2024) bounds PC in specified complete-mediator process models, not an arbitrary M-Y-confounded response polytope.
Gabriel, Sachs, and Sjolander (2023) demonstrates sharp canonical-confounder mediation bounds for different risk-difference targets.

Xie and Li (2026, Theorem 3.3) combines a back-door set and mediator conditional-independence constraints in a PNS program, but does not retain arbitrary M-Y response dependence or characterize a monotonicity strictness frontier.
Zhang, Wang, and Miao (2025) studies sharp PN-bound inference with treatment-outcome data and optional covariates, but not mediator-law envelopes or a monotonicity-gain map.
Rubinstein, Cuellar, and Malinsky (2025) identifies mediated PC quantities under stronger cross-world conditions.
Kawakami and Tian (2025) identifies path-specific PNS measures rather than an observable monotonicity frontier for PN.
method_exemplars_used: Balke and Pearl (1997): response-type simplex, all observed-cell equalities, and primal-dual sharpness.
Fang and Santos (2019): Hadamard directional derivative and modified bootstrap at finite-envelope ties.
literature_map.writing_exemplar_used: Dawid, Murtas, and Musio (2014): experimental attribution bounds, then the incremental information supplied by an observed mediator.

novelty_justification: The closest published results are Tian and Pearl (2000), which provides generic sharp PN bounds, Xie and Li (2026), which supplies a mediator-informed program under back-door and conditional-independence constraints, and Dawid, Murtas, and Musio (2014), which exploits a mediator only without mediator-outcome confounding. None gives an observable if-and-only-if characterization of strict contraction at both endpoints caused by deleting mediator-defier types from the complete confounded response-type polytope. The proposed threshold is not a re-expression of the endpoint gaps: it is the minimum defier mass on each independently defined sign-free extremal face. The open claim is to prove its equivalence to positive endpoint gains and derive a perturbation-stable compatible regime.
